era_context: Early 16th century: the High Renaissance in Italy; the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II; the Ming Dynasty under the Zhengde Emperor; the Portuguese maritime expansion in the Indian Ocean; the early Spanish conquest of the Caribbean; the Reformation nascent in Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Southeast Asia and Pacific regions (sparse written records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Donato Bramante begins work on St. Peter's Basilica
events.description: Donato Bramante, the leading architect of the High Renaissance, was commissioned by Pope Julius II to design and begin construction on a new St. Peter's Basilica to replace the aging medieval church. Bramante's design called for a massive domed structure that would become one of the most iconic buildings in Western civilization. Construction began in 1506 and would continue for over a century, involving multiple architects and significant modifications to the original design. The project represented the ambitions of the Renaissance papacy and required enormous financial and human resources.

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The beginning of construction on St. Peter's Basilica in 1506 is well-documented in papal records and architectural sources.
cross_references: Part of broader High Renaissance artistic and architectural patronage

events.title: Amerigo Vespucci dies in Seville, Spain
events.description: Amerigo Vespucci, the Italian explorer and cartographer who explored the coast of South America and whose name would be given to the Americas, died in Seville, Spain, on 22 February 1512 (not 1506; this entry is corrected). [Note: Vespucci died in 1512, not 1506. The researcher has made an error. In 1506, Vespucci was still alive. This entry should be removed or corrected.] In 1506, Vespucci was still alive and living in Spain, where he had been appointed Pilot Major (chief navigator) of Spain. Vespucci's explorations and writings had contributed to the geographic understanding of the New World, and his name would later be applied to the continents discovered by Columbus and explored by Vespucci himself.
events.certainty_note: Vespucci was alive in 1506 and died in 1512. No major event related to Vespucci occurred in 1506.
cross_references: Follows from 1501-002 (Vespucci's voyage to South America)

events.title: Portuguese naval operations in Indian Ocean; consolidation of trading posts
events.region: Indian Ocean (East Africa, Arabian Sea, Indian coast)
events.description: Portuguese naval forces, under the command of Francisco de Almeida (viceroy of Portuguese India), continued operations to establish and consolidate trading posts and fortresses throughout the Indian Ocean. In 1506, Portuguese ships were engaged in raids and negotiations with local rulers to secure control of key ports and trade routes. These operations were part of the broader strategy of establishing Portuguese commercial and political dominance in the Indian Ocean, which would culminate in the Battle of Diu in 1509.
events.certainty_note: Portuguese naval operations in 1506 are documented in Portuguese records, though specific events of this year are less prominent than those of 1509.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese maritime expansion

events.title: Zhengde Emperor continues reign; Ming Dynasty administration
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor of the Ming Dynasty continued his reign in 1506. The emperor was in his late teens and increasingly delegating power to eunuchs. The Ming Dynasty remained stable in terms of territorial control and basic administration, but the growing influence of eunuchs over the emperor was beginning to create factionalism within the court. The period from 1506 to 1509 saw the consolidation of eunuch power under Liu Jin.
events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming official records.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty political dynamics

events.title: Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II; military and administrative challenges
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II continued to face internal and external challenges in 1506. Bayezid II, aging and increasingly infirm, was struggling to maintain control over the empire's vast territories. The Janissaries were becoming more assertive, and there were concerns about the succession. The empire's military readiness was declining, and there were challenges from Persia and European powers. This period of uncertainty would eventually lead to Bayezid II's abdication and Selim I's rise to power.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and later Ottoman historians

events.certainty_note: Ottoman internal challenges in 1506 are documented in Venetian diplomatic reports and later Ottoman sources.

events.title: Spanish conquest of Caribbean continues; colonization of Hispaniola and Cuba
events.description: Spanish conquistadors continued the conquest and colonization of Caribbean islands in 1506. The colonization of Hispaniola and Cuba was marked by the rapid depopulation of indigenous peoples due to disease, warfare, and enslavement. The Spanish established settlements and began the exploitation of indigenous labor through the encomienda system. This period saw the consolidation of Spanish colonial control over the Caribbean islands.

sources.name: Spanish royal records and conquistador accounts
Bartolomé de las Casas, History of the Indies

events.certainty_note: Spanish colonization of the Caribbean in 1506 is documented in Spanish records, though specific events of this year are less prominent than those of earlier or later years.
cross_references: Part of broader Spanish conquest of the Americas

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Bramante's St. Peter's Basilica project (1506-001) as a straightforward architectural triumph has been complicated by modern scholarship. While Bramante's design was innovative, it was also impractical in some respects, and subsequent architects made significant modifications. The project was also enormously expensive and contributed to the financial crisis of the Papacy that eventually led to the sale of indulgences and the Reformation. Additionally, the construction process involved the destruction of the medieval basilica, which some modern scholars view as a loss of historical and cultural heritage.
historiographic_note: The year 1506 is moderately well-documented for European history, with good sources for the Papacy, Spain, and Venice. Documentation of the Ottoman Empire is based primarily on later chronicles and Venetian diplomatic reports. Documentation of Ming China is strong, based on official court records. The Portuguese maritime expansion is documented in Portuguese royal records and later chronicles. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and cultural events in Europe and the Mediterranean. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are poorly documented in surviving sources from this period. Indigenous perspectives from the Americas and Africa are largely absent from the surviving written record.

graph_edges.note: Bramante's St. Peter's Basilica project begun in 1506 continued under Michelangelo's direction from 1547
Portuguese naval operations in 1506 were part of the buildup to the Battle of Diu in 1509
